Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the Abbott International Symposium on glycemic control. It is a real pleasure to open this session. Over the next two days, researchers from many countries will present their work, share their findings and discuss the future of clinical nutrition together. Let me explain how the symposium works, so you can make the most of it. First, each speaker has fifteen minutes to present a study. Please structure your talk clearly: open with your objective, walk us through your methods, show your key results, and finish with strong closing remarks. Second, after each talk, there is a short panel. The panel is your chance to defend your data and answer tough questions from experts in the room. Stay calm, acknowledge good points, stand by your strong evidence, and be honest about any limitation. Third, in the final session, we ask every speaker to synthesize the main ideas of the whole symposium. The goal is to integrate the different studies into one clear message for the field. Remember, this is an international audience, so adapt your message, read the directness of each question, and show cultural sensitivity. We are all here to learn from one another. So present with confidence, advocate for good science, and enjoy the symposium.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the clinical team. I am calling about your slot at the international symposium tomorrow. I just wanted to wish you luck and give you a few last tips before you present. First, on structure. Keep your talk simple and clear: open with the objective, explain the methods, show the main results, and end with strong closing remarks. You have done this many times now, so trust yourself. Second, on the panel. After your talk, the panel will ask questions, and some colleagues are very direct, so do not take it personally. If they challenge the sample size, acknowledge the point, then defend your data with the robust results you have. If you do not know something, just say you will get back to them. Third, in the closing session, they will ask you to synthesize the main findings of the symposium and integrate them into one message. Think about the big picture, not only your own study. And remember everything you learned about cross-cultural communication: it is an international audience, so adapt your message and show sensitivity. You are ready for this, Patricia. Go and present with confidence, advocate for your work, and enjoy it. Call me afterwards and tell me how it went.

First, present your study clearly: open with the objective, explain the methods and show the key results. Next, finish your talk with strong closing remarks that leave one clear message. After that, join the panel and defend your data, acknowledging good points and standing by your strong evidence. Then, in the final session, synthesize the main findings of the whole symposium. Finally, integrate the different studies into one message and advocate for good science to the international audience.
